title: ARP Protocol — Address Resolution Process
examTip: ARP resolves Layer 3 (IP) to Layer 2 (MAC). ARP request is broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF), reply is unicast. `arp -a` shows ARP table. Gratuitous ARP: host broadcasts its own IP→MAC to update neighbors' caches (used after IP change). Proxy ARP: router answers on behalf of hosts on another subnet.

messages.label: Check for MAC of 192.168.1.5
messages.note:
Host A needs to send a frame
to 192.168.1.5 but doesn't know
the MAC address. Checks local cache.

messages.label: Cache Miss (not found)
messages.note:
192.168.1.5 not in ARP table.
Must send ARP Request to
discover MAC address.

messages.label: ARP Request BROADCAST: "Who has 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.1"
messages.note:
Ethernet frame:
Dest MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF
Src MAC = Host A's MAC
Opcode = 1 (Request)
All hosts on subnet receive this

messages.label: Receives broadcast
messages.note:
All hosts receive the broadcast.
Only host with IP 192.168.1.5
(Host B) will respond.

messages.label: ARP Reply (UNICAST): "192.168.1.5 is at AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF"
messages.note:
Sent UNICAST directly to Host A.
Opcode = 2 (Reply)
Contains Host B's MAC address
AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF

messages.label: Store mapping: 192.168.1.5 → AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF
messages.note:
Mapping stored in ARP cache
with a timeout (typically 20 min
for dynamic entries). Avoids
repeated ARP broadcasts.

messages.label: Begin normal communication using MAC address
messages.note:
Host A now frames its data
with dest MAC AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF.
Layer 2 (Ethernet) communication
proceeds normally.

notes: ARP operates at Layer 2/3 boundary — maps IP (L3) to MAC (L2)
Gratuitous ARP: host sends unsolicited ARP reply of its own IP to update caches
Proxy ARP: router responds to ARP requests on behalf of hosts on other subnets
ARP Spoofing attack: attacker sends fake ARP replies to poison caches (MITM attack)
theory:
ARP (Address Resolution Protocol), defined in RFC 826 (1982), is a network communication protocol used to discover the hardware (MAC) address of a device from its known IP address within a local network. ARP operates at the boundary of Layer 2 (Data Link) and Layer 3 (Network) of the OSI model.

Why ARP is Needed: IP addresses (Layer 3) are logical addresses used for routing across networks. However, actual data frame delivery on a local network (LAN) requires Layer 2 MAC addresses. When a host knows the IP address of the destination but not its MAC address, it uses ARP to discover it. Without ARP, Ethernet frames cannot be correctly addressed for local delivery.

ARP Request — Broadcast: The requesting host constructs an ARP Request packet with its own IP and MAC as the sender fields, and the target IP address as the destination. Since the target MAC is unknown, the Ethernet frame is broadcast to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF — every device on the LAN receives and processes this. The ARP packet contains opcode 1 (Request).

ARP Reply — Unicast: The host that owns the target IP address sends an ARP Reply directly (unicast) back to the requester. The reply contains the sender's MAC address. All other hosts simply discard the broadcast. The ARP packet contains opcode 2 (Reply).

ARP Cache: To avoid repeating ARP lookups for every packet, hosts maintain an ARP cache (ARP table) — a temporary mapping of IP addresses to MAC addresses. Dynamic entries typically expire after 20 minutes (varies by OS). The command `arp -a` displays the current ARP table. Static ARP entries can be configured manually for security.

Special ARP Types: Gratuitous ARP — a host broadcasts an ARP reply of its own IP-to-MAC mapping, used after IP address changes or NIC replacement to update neighbors' ARP caches. Proxy ARP — a router responds to ARP requests for hosts on other subnets, enabling inter-subnet communication without the host knowing.

ARP Security Issues: ARP has no authentication mechanism, making it vulnerable to ARP Spoofing (ARP Poisoning) attacks where an attacker sends forged ARP replies to associate their MAC with another host's IP, enabling Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks. Defenses include Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches and static ARP entries for critical hosts.
keyPoints: ARP maps Layer 3 IP addresses to Layer 2 MAC addresses within a local subnet
ARP Request is BROADCAST (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) — all hosts on subnet receive it
ARP Reply is UNICAST — only the requesting host receives the reply
ARP cache stores IP→MAC mappings temporarily (default ~20 min) to reduce broadcast traffic
Gratuitous ARP: host broadcasts its own IP→MAC mapping to update neighbors' caches proactively
ARP Spoofing is a common MITM attack; Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches prevents it
useCases: Local network communication — any time two hosts on the same subnet communicate, ARP resolves IP to MAC
Default gateway resolution — end devices use ARP to find the MAC address of their router/gateway
Network troubleshooting — ARP table inspection (`arp -a`) helps diagnose Layer 2 connectivity issues and detect ARP spoofing attacks
